turns.content: I'm following along with a talk on Rust error handling and I need help mapping out the logic for collecting errors from multiple services. How do I combine a Vec<Result<SomeType>> into a single Result<Vec<SomeType>> so that if any service fails, the entire operation fails?

You should use the try operator or the iterator collection method to map over your vector and collect results. You can call vec::try_iter() on a Vec<Result<T>> which returns an Iterator<Item=Result<T>> that you can then collect into a Result<Vec<T>> using the collect() pattern, but this only works if all items succeed; otherwise it propagates the first error encountered.

That sounds good for fail-fast behavior. But what if I want to know which service actually failed instead of just stopping at the first one? Can I still use try_iter() or do I need a different approach like using Option::collect()? I'm trying to decide between returning an empty vector and storing partial successes.

You cannot use try_iter() if you want to preserve all errors because it aborts immediately on the first failure. Instead, you should collect into a Vec<Result<T>> and then manually process them using match or if-let chains to separate successful results from failed ones. If you need a Result<Vec<T>>, you can implement your own custom error type that includes both the list of successes and the list of failures.

That makes sense, but I'm worried about complexity. Is there a way to avoid writing this logic manually? Can I leverage something like serde_json::Value or std::error::Error traits to automatically handle the grouping without implementing my own custom types?

There is no built-in trait that automatically converts a Vec<Result<T>> into Result<Vec<T>> while preserving all errors, so you will need to write some logic manually. The std::error::Error trait defines how to format error messages but does not provide methods for collecting multiple errors into a single Result. You must implement your own logic using the From trait or manual iteration if you want to reuse existing error types from crates like thiserror.

Okay, so I'll need to manually iterate and collect successes into one vector and failures into another. But what about fail-last vs fail-first? If I use try_iter(), it's inherently fail-first. How do I implement a scenario where only the last failure matters?

You can achieve fail-last by collecting all errors into a vector first, then checking if any exist before returning an error containing that list, or you could modify your collection logic to append each new error only if the previous one did not succeed. For fail-first behavior with try_iter(), it's straightforward but for fail-last you need to handle the accumulation explicitly in your code.

Thanks, that clarifies things. One last thing: if I'm building a CLI tool using clap, how does this pattern interact with clap::Error? Should I use the Error type or just plain Result for my internal logic before returning to the CLI?

You should keep your internal logic as Result<Vec<T>> and convert it to a custom error structure that implements std::error::Error and Display traits, then construct a clap::Error from that. This allows you to maintain fine-grained control over which errors are captured and how they are reported without letting clap interfere with your collection logic.
I'm struggling with how to handle errors in my async data fetching pipeline. I want to collect a vector of results where each one might fail, but I need the entire batch to succeed or fail together.

You are likely thinking about using Option::collect() on your Vec<Option<T>> which is great for success-only cases, but that won't help you propagate individual errors. For a collection of results where any failure stops the whole process, you should use Result::from_iter() or iterate with collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>(). The key distinction is whether you want fail-first (stop at first error) versus fail-last (aggregate all errors into one).
I see. So if I have a Vec<Result<T, E>>, can I just call collect() and get a Result<Vec<T>, E>?

Almost, but you need to be careful with the error type. If you use collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>(), it will only propagate the first error encountered into the final Result. That is fail-first behavior which might not match your needs if you want to know exactly which items failed. To combine errors from multiple sources or get a single aggregated error, you would need to chain operations with and_then() or use fold() to collect all E values.
Okay, so I can't just use collect() if I want to see the specific failures. What's the best pattern for a batch of async fetches?

For async batch operations where you want fail-first behavior but still track what went wrong, you should iterate through your collection manually with futures::stream or manual loops. If you use the standard library's collect() on a stream of Results, it stops at the first failure. To get all errors while stopping execution, you can map each result to Result<T, E> and then use fold() to accumulate errors if needed, or simply return early once an error is found in your loop logic.
Wait, I thought collect() would give me a vector of all results even if some failed?

That's the misconception. Result::collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>() is designed to return either success with all values or failure with just the first error E. It does not preserve intermediate failures in the output vector. If you need to keep failed items alongside successful ones, you cannot use Result<Vec<T>>; instead you should collect into Vec<Result<T, E>> and handle that pattern yourself.
So I have to manually check each item and stop? That seems inefficient for large batches.

You are correct that a full manual loop is expensive if you only care about stopping early. However, there is a more efficient approach using the std::iter module with fold(). You can iterate over your stream and use fold() to collect errors up until failure while maintaining performance characteristics similar to a simple for loop.
Can you show me how to implement this fail-first logic without collecting every error?

You can use the iterator's collect method with a specific closure or simply use the standard library's from_iter() pattern but wrap it in conditional logic. The most idiomatic way is to map your async items into Results, then call collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>() which gives you fail-first behavior automatically without manual error accumulation.
I'm still confused about the difference between Vec<Result<T>> and Result<Vec<T>>. Which one should I use for my production code?

For production code where you want to know if the entire batch succeeded, Result<Vec<T>, E> is appropriate because it propagates a single failure reason without needing manual error aggregation. If your business logic requires detailed reporting of which specific requests failed, then stick with Vec<Result<T>> and handle each error individually in your application layer instead of trying to force them into a single Result.
